behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Failure risk is at the parser+normalizer+correlator boundary (field mapping, service-specific schema differences). A pure unit test on grouping logic can miss wiring errors where trace_id is parsed inconsistently across modules.
behaviors.test_description: Feed mixed JSON logs from at least 3 service schemas through the real ingestion pipeline (no mocks for parsing/normalization), then assert all records sharing a trace_id appear in one correlated request group independent of service name.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on timestamp parsing, skew compensation, and timeline assembly across modules. A unit test of sort alone may pass while real pipeline ordering fails due to parse/normalization differences.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end timeline assembly on fixture logs from multiple services with controlled skew values (including ±500ms edge cases), then assert rendered order matches expected causal sequence after skew adjustment.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is metric aggregation + percentile computation + anomaly reporter wiring. A unit test for percentile math alone could miss baseline-window or report-threshold integration bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Inject log streams representing stable baseline latency then a spike period via the real aggregation pipeline, and assert anomaly output flags the service/request when computed p99 exceeds 3x baseline.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: Need real parser error behavior and pipeline error-handling path. Mocking parser exceptions can hide actual failure modes (partial reads, decoder exceptions, counter not propagated).
behaviors.test_description: Process a batch containing valid and malformed JSON lines through real ingestion/parsing modules; assert pipeline completes, valid entries are preserved, malformed entries are skipped, and reported skipped-count equals malformed-count.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: This depends on runtime scheduling, I/O buffering, queue backpressure, and throughput under load. In-process mocks can easily hide drop/lag behavior, so real process-level load is required.
behaviors.test_description: Run the tool as a real subprocess and drive it with a load generator at 10k entries/sec for a sustained interval; measure ingest vs processed counts, queue lag, and end-to-end delay, and fail on any drop or sustained backlog growth.
